clinical_details: History of prostate carcinoma/ 6/52 history of drop in visual acuity and choroidal mass noted with retinal detachment. Trans-vitreal biopsy.

2.9cm thyroid cyst, 1mm fibrous capsule
Hyperparathyroidism due to chronic renal failure. Unable to find parathyroid glands at surgery. Thyroidectomy. 2 pale nodules seen grossly.

Retroperitoneal mass attached to the right adrenal gland. Biochemical tests negative for phaeochromocytoma. Incidentaloma. Macro: A nodular mass 4cm in maximum dimension attached to adrenal gland, together 46g. Cut surface is yellow-tan and whorled. The tissue between the nodule and the adrenal gland shows myxoid appearance.

Subtotal thyroidectomy. Follicular cells on FNA (Thy 3). Macro: Subtotal thyroidectomy specimen 21g, 6 x 4.5 x 1.5cm. Sectioning showed an ill-defined whitish area approximately 1.5 x 1.5 x 1.1cm. Immunohistochemistry: CD20, CD23 and CD5 positive in an adjacent small lymph node.
diagnosis: Follicular variant of papillary carcinoma, with small lymphocytic lymphoma (CLL) in the adjacent lymph node

clinical_details: Cystic mass identified in right adrenal on scan. Adrenalectomy performed.
Had subtotal thyroidectomy comprising right lobe, part of the left lobe and isthmus. The specimen weighs 30g. The cut surface has a multinodular appearance with pale bands of fibrosis.

The adrenal gland is completely replaced by a tumour measuring 10.0 x 8.5 x 4.0cm and weighs 220gms. The cut surface is variegated with areas of haemorrhage and necrosis. The capsule appears intact. The cells are stained positively with Vimentin and cytokeratin 7, but showed no staining with CEA, EMA and cytokeratin 20. Chromogranin and synaptophysin were negative.
Nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid. Patient had a long-standing history of hypothyroidism.
diagnosis: Follicular variant of papillary thyroid carcinoma

clinical_details: History of thyrotoxicosis and radioactive iodine therapy.

Back and loin pain. Huge left adrenal mass displacing kidney, spleen and pancreas. Previous breast cancer. Left adrenalectomy performed. Macro: an enlarged adrenal gland with attached kidney weighing together 1360g.....The adrenal gland was attached to the upper pole/medial surface of the kidney measuring 160x100x90mm. Cut surface showed a variegated appearance with pale and dark areas and areas of necrosis. The gland was covered by a thin white capsule. Micro: The lesion was a tumour comprising medium sized polygonal cells with occasional foci of residual adrenal cortex at the edges. There was brisk mitotic activity, and no capsular invasion was seen. Immunohistochemistry: CK20, CK7 and EMA +ve, and weakly +ve with PGP9.5 and synaptophysin. Melan A, inhibin, chromogranin, ER and PR -ve, occasional cells S100+ve, but no sustentacular cells, occasional nuclei TTF1 +ve, Ki67 index around 50%, BRST 1 -ve.

Solitary nodule in left lobe of thyroid, FNA THY4, ? Papillary carcinoma, left thyroid lobectomy. Micro: This lesion was submitted with a proffered diagnosis of minimally invasive follicular carcinoma. It comprised a well circumscribed follicular nodule with a thick fibrous capsule. There was also one prominent focus of capsular invasion with associated vascular invasion. The nuclei were optically clear and most members thought that these nuclei showed nuclear features of papillary carcinoma. Most members thought this was a FVPC with microinvasion, a few members also considered follicular carcinoma with microinvasion, or follicular adenoma
diagnosis: Minimally invasive follicular variant of papillary carcinoma

clinical_details: Previous radioiodine therapy for thyrotoxicosis. Nodular gland, FNA suggestive of oncocytic follicular neoplasm.

Thyroid nodule. USS showed large nodule in right lobe and two small nodules in left. Right lobectomy performed. Also had had a recent nephrectomy. Pre-operative US core biopsy cytology of the thyroid showed of the right lobe had shown a lesion that could have been a metastatic RCC or could have been a follicular thyroid lesion., thy4, hence diagnostic hemithyroidectomy. The cytology showed cells that were thyroglobulin +/-, Cd10-ve and RCC1 -ve. Proferred diagnosis was of intrathyroidal metastasis of conventional (clear cell) RCC.
diagnosis: Metastatic Conventional (Clear Cell) Renal Cell Carcinoma

clinical_details: Nodular left lobe of thyroid, lobectomy following non-diagnostic cytology.

Cushing's syndrome, right adrenalectomy. Additional History - increasing weight. Specimen of left adrenal gland once stripped of fat measuring 60 x 35 x 35mm and weighing 18g with a small ragged defect in the capsule at one edge measuring 20 x 4mm in area. Gland had a bright yellow nodular appearance, no rim of normal adrenal tissue identified. Microscopically there was expansion of the zona fasiculata cells with a smaller amount of zona reticularis. Nest of cortical cells were separated by thin fibrous bands, with areas of mild to moderate nuclear pleomorphism. Mitoses less than 0/50 HPF. Proffered diagnosis was of adrenal cortical hyperplasia rather than cortical adenoma. The cells were large/expanded and lipid laden and that gland capsule was well defined with a mixture of clear cells and compact cells and that NO defined nodule was present. The lipid laden cells were present because the patient was receiving adrenal gland blockade. No nodular areas were present and the lesion showed expansile growth.
diagnosis: Adrenal in Pituitary Dependent Cushing's Syndrome

clinical_details: Right upper quadrant pain. CT - adrenal mass, non-functioning. Micro: Yellow mass 10 x 75 x 50mm with a smooth intact surface and adjacent attenuated adrenal gland 75 x 15 x 10mm, weighing 110g.
Cushing's syndrome. Adrenal tumour. Adrenalectomy.
Left adrenal lesion. Fever, weight loss. ?Abscess on CT. ?TB.
Lymphadenopathy right side of neck
Enlarged right submandibular gland. FNAC suggestive of adenoid cystic carcinoma.
Male, aged 80's, incidental right thyroid lump, 30mm solid and cystic lesion replacing most of right lobe.
diagnosis: A range of differential diagnoses were offered in this case from follicular adenoma, FVPC, poorly differentiated carcinoma and papillary carcinoma with dedifferentiation. There was lack of agreement in this case as to the final diagnosis, although the proffered diagnosis was poorly differentiated thyroid carcinoma. Vascular invasion was present in this case in other sections although it was not evident in the slide circulated

clinical_details: Female, aged in 40's, 15mm thyroid lesion, thyroid showed multinodular goitre, weighed 131g.
diagnosis: This was another controversial case. A range of diagnoses was offered including medullary carcinoma, follicular adenoma, insular carcinoma, and poorly differentiated follicular carcinoma. The proffered diagnosis was incidental insular carcinoma of the thyroid

clinical_details: Female, aged in 50's. Left adrenal heterogeneous mass weighing 352g with cystic and solid areas.
diagnosis: Most members diagnosed this as a phaeochromocytoma, although some correctly commented on its adverse prognostic features.

clinical_details: Female in 70's, previous partial thyroidectomy, subsequent ? local recurrence and evidence of metastatic disease with large biopsy proven metastasis in right clavicle. Known lung metastases. One dose of I 131 5 months previously. Block of normal looking completion thyroidectomy
diagnosis: All members thought that this thyroid gland showed florid changes in keeping with radioiodine therapy.

clinical_details: Right hemithyroidectomy for 40mm solitary nodule.
diagnosis: Most members thought that this was a FVPC on the basis of the circulated slide, however the submitting pathologists were able to show that the lesion was C k19 -ve, and was thus after discussion reclassified as a follicular adenoma.

clinical_details: Male in 60's, mass located high in carotid sheath, for 12 years weighing 48g.
diagnosis: Members though that this was a parathyroid adenoma with clear cell (water clear) change, other diagnoses offered were paraganglioma and possible parathyroid carcinoma, or metastatic renal cell carcinoma. The proffered diagnosis was parathyroid adenoma with water clear change

clinical_details: Female late 40's, thyroid lobectomy
diagnosis: The proffered diagnosis in this case was worrisome histologic alterations following fine-needle aspiration of the thyroid (WHAFFT) (1) with pseudopapillary hyperplasia although there was a range of differential diagnoses given, most were follicular adenoma and some thought that the clear cell change present could indicate a primary thyroid follicular carcinoma or metastatic renal cell carcinoma

clinical_details: Female late 70's parathyroid excision.
diagnosis: Most diagnoses were of oncocytic parathyroid adenoma, with a few diagnosing a paraganglioma. This lesion while quite atypical in its cytology did not show malignant features.

clinical_details: Female early 30's parathyroidectomy 3 years ago, now continuing hyperparathyroidism, tissue in scar and mid trachea level VI. Tissue taken for frozen section.
diagnosis: Virtually all the diagnoses were of benign parathyroid implants/ parathyromatosis with a few also considering parathyroid carcinoma in the differential diagnosis. This case had originally been diagnosed a parathyroid carcinoma and a radical neck dissection performed. It was only after there was a recurrence in the thorax on mediastinoscopy that the final diagnosis of benign implantation due to secondary hyperparathyroidism was made.

clinical_details: Male late 70's. 46mm pancreatic tumour, recent left nephrectomy for oncocytoma ? second primary (CA 19.9=115)
diagnosis: Grade 1 endocrine tumour (WHO) with amyloid/pancreatic endocrine neoplasm. This tumour was CD 56, chromogranin, synaptophysin and NSE+ve and insulin lucagon, somatostatin, VIP, gastric and calcitonin -ve. Ki67 proliferation index <2%. This indicated a tumour of Beta cell phenotype.

clinical_details: Female mid 60's, left thyroid mass, cytology follicular, left thyroid lobectomy weighing 39g with a mass occupying most of the lobe.
diagnosis: Most members thought that this was a poorly differentiated carcinoma/insular carcinoma with vascular invasion although others thought it was a neuroendocrine or follicular carcinoma. The neuroendocrine markers; calcitonin, chromogranin and Cd56 were negative, and Ck19 was largely negative in the tumour nuclei.

clinical_details: Sex not given, age early 50's, Cushing's syndrome, bilateral adrenal masses with imaging suggesting macronodular hyperplasia. Left adrenalectomy was154g and 51mm in size. Cut surface homogeneous yellow without haemorrhage or necrosis. Circumscribed margins with multiple nodules evident 2 H+E slides C and E.
diagnosis: The proffered diagnosis in this case was macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (with marked adrenal enlargement) (2). It was pointed out that the sections showed diffuse hyperplasia of all the cortical layers, with some areas showing pseudoglandular spaces and adrenal extending out into adjacent adipose tissue (both features are described in this entity). It has been postulated that this ACTH independent cause of Cushing's syndrome is due to aberrant adrenal receptors (3)

clinical_details: Female mid 50's, firm nodule in thyroid isthmus 14mm in size.
diagnosis: Most members thought this was papillary carcinoma of the thyroid, some saying that this was the oncocytic variant, others tall cell, others columnar cell. It was commented that the architectural and cytological features apart from the nuclear features were those of papillary carcinoma.

clinical_details: Nodule left thyroid.
Large multinodular goitre. Right hemithyroidectomy. Macro: homogenous greyish brown tissue throughout. CK19 positive.
diagnosis: Follicular variant of papillary thyroid carcinoma

clinical_details: Nodular thyroid. Normal free T4, slight increase in TSH, cytology Thy 3. Left thyroid lobe. Nodular on sectioning.

clinical_details: Frozen section (re-cut from block). Clinical history: Suspicious right thyroid nodule; right vocal cord palsy. Specimen consists of three small pieces of firm tissue, the largest 7mm in diameter.

A 59 year-old woman noticed enlargement of the thyroid gland. Fine needle aspiration was not conclusive for the diagnosis. A right hemithyroidectomy was performed, showing multiple, well-circumscribed, solid, bright orange-red nodules ranging from 0.8 to 3.5cm. The surrounding thyroid parenchyma was tan-red and fleshy.

A 63 year-old man presented with a large nodule in his right lobe of the thyroid gland. Fine needle aspiration contained atypical follicular cells. The right hemithyroidectomy was performed. There was a 5.5cm distinct mass which had infiltrative growth macroscopically.
Right thyroid nodule and right level IV node. ?Metastatic thyroid carcinoma. FNA thyroid.
Female late 40's, right thyroid lobectomy with a 23mm solid nodule.

clinical_details: Enlarging right adrenal mass 5cm in diameter. Past history of melanoma. No known secondary elsewhere ? Melanoma ? other tumour. R hemithyroidectomy. Specimen: R thyroid lobe 60 x 40 x 35 mm containing a well-defined, 40 mm nodule with homogenous, tan-coloured, solid cut surface. No cystic, haemorrhagic or necrotic areas. The nodule occupies the lower 2/3rd of the lobe. Rest of thyroid appeared normal. Special stains/IHC results: None.

Left lobe of thyroid. Hypoechoic lesion. Radiology U4. FNA Thy3f. Specimen: A left hemithyroidectomy specimen measuring 70 x 36 x 25 mm. The lower and middle pole is occupied by a solitary, circumscribed and fairly demarcated thinly rimmed pale yellow nodule measuring 32 x 28 x 20 mm. The cut surface of the nodule is pale yellow with areas of haemorrhage and fibrosis. The nodule almost abuts the capsule. Special stains/IHC results: IHC - negative for Calcitonin. Focal vascular invasion and capsular disruption noted in another section.

Right hemi-thyroidectomy. Right thyroid U3, Thy3f. Specimen: Hemi-thyroid 21 g, 24 x 57 x 28 mm. There is a nodule 47 mm across which abuts the capsule. The background thyroid appears normal. Representative section.

Special stains/IHC results: CEA+, CD56+, chromogranin+, synaptophysin+, TTF1 patchy+, PAX8-, HBME1-. Pale eosinophilic material is Congo Red positive with apple green birefringence.

Tail of pancreas tumour ? Ductal adenocarcinoma, ? NET. Specimen: Tail of pancreas specimen containing a 42 mm tumour.

Special stains/IHC results: Tumour cells positive for AE1/3, CAIX, PAX8, RCC marker, CD10. Negative for synaptophysin, chromogranin, beta-catenin.
diagnosis: Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma

History of menorrhagia, followed by an attempt at microwave endometrial ablation. This procedure was unsuccessful and the patient was operated on with a view to removing her uterus. At operation there was an unexpected finding of fleshy tissue on the surface of the uterus, adnexae and peritoneal structures. The omentum also appeared abnormal and felt thickened. Nephrotic syndrome. Haematuria but normal creatinine. Immunoperoxidase: IgA-, IgG- (but ?reliable), IgM++, C1q++, C3b+, C4 trace, all mesangial and peripheral.
Recurrent haematuria, proteinuria 4.4gms, creatinine clearance 62ml/min. IF showed IgG-, IgA++ (mesangial), C3 non-specific, fibrin.
Presented with nephrotic syndrome. Longstanding diabetes mellitus. IF probably non-contributory; some granular mesangial IgM and linear IgA.
Progressive renal impairment over days, creatinine 350, pANCA positive, MPO>1000, complains of myalgia, night sweats. History of peripheral vascular disease. IF: flecks of IgM and C3 in glomeruli.
Presenting in renal failure with macroscopic haematuria, dialysis dependent. Immunology including anti-GBM antibodies negative. History of previous extensive DVT.

Blood and protein on dipstick. Creatinine 69. ?grade of renal involvement. Diagnosis is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE); please grade activity by WHO scheme. (To be discussed by Dr I.Roberts at Participants' Meeting) Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) diagnosed 1996.
diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus, WHO grade III

clinical_details: Acute renal bleed. Hb down to 6. CAT - large haematoma lower pole left kidney. Tumour circulation on angiogram.

Hypertension (160/100) and proteinuria 7g found one year ago. Treated with ACE inhibitor. BP dropped to 140/90, proteinuria fell to 3.5g. Normal creatinine. Ultrasound of kidneys normal. IF: Granular capillary basement membrane IgG++ with C3+, some IgA in tubules. No EM.
Abdominal pain; elevated serum creatinine level (4.25mg/dl); microhaematuria; proteinuria of 0.9mg/dl; oliguria (500ml/24h); anaemia; history of continuous malignant hypertension.
Five days cough and sputum followed by gross oedema. Creatinine 152. ASO titre equivocal. EM not yet available.
Polyneuropathy, polyarthropathy, vasculitic rash affecting lower limbs, protein+, blood+, ANCA negative, raised IgA, creatinine 140. IF - mesangial IgA and C3.
Multisystem disease with renal involvement, sinusitis, pyrexia, blood+, protein+ ?vasculitis. Immunostaining negative.

Malaria following trip to Nigeria (low parasitaemia). Presented with creatinine 1200 with no previous renal problems. Urine 4+ blood, 4+ protein. HIV serology performed following biopsy - negative. Hyalinotic resorption droplets within podocytes are positive with IgG, IgA and C3 but these antibodies are otherwise negative. There is weak focal positivity for IgM (+/-) in glomerular capillary walls.
Sweating, high ESR (126mm/hr), polyuria, polydipsia, ANCA negative. CRP<6, creatinine 250.

Acute renal failure,?cause (probably secondary to sepsis). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) 1:320 (speckled), ANCA, anti-GBM negative. Decreased immunoglobulins on serum electrophoresis. Clinical signs of systemic sclerosis but not hypertensive. Treated with inotropes for hypotension and subsequently improved. Subsequent enquiry: she became acutely hypertensive, following administration of steroids at another hospital, went into ARF. Had definite clin diagnosis of scleroderma. She remains dialysis dependent.

Sudden onset of deafness. Pulmonary opacity noted on CXR. Haematuria and proteinuria at presentation. Renal function normal. ANCA positive. Immunofluorescence - deposition of C3 and IgG in areas of fibrinoid necrosis, otherwise negative.

New acute renal failure. Urea 75, creatinine 2187. Oliguric. Normal sized kidneys. Antibody status not known. Two pulses of methyl prednisolone already given. Immunofluorescence: IgA, C1q negative. IgM +/- rather "smudged" basement membrane staining. IgG, C3 +++ linear basement membrane positivity. C4 -/+ linear basement membrane.

Nephrotic syndrome. Severe ascites, albumin 34, creatinine 165. Enlarged spleen. Normal liver bx and duodenal bx. Normal serum lipids and liver function tests. Normochromic, normocytic anaemia 9. Borderline hypopituitarism - normal pituitary on CT. Low T4, raised prolactin and impotence. Freckles on lips no other evidence of Peutz-Jegher syndrome. Nerve conduction studies - evidence of polyneuropathy. Nephrotic syndrome responded to steroids. IF - diffuse mesangial A,G,M,C3.
Previous history of cutaneous lymphoma diagnosed four years ago. Acute renal failure with fever and enlarged kidneys.

Presented with acute renal failure. History of Raynaud's syndrome and skin changes, ?connective tissue disorder. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) positive. Rheumatoid factor positive, double stranded DNA antibodies negative. Other auto-antibodies pending. Blood pressure mildly elevated. Platelets normal. Immunofluorescence showed no evidence of glomerular deposition of IgG, IgM, IgA, C3 or C4.

Diabetic for many years with retinopathy. 8g proteinuria. Normal renal function. Duplex kidney. IF: Small amount of granular staining for C3 in the mesangium near the hilum. C3, IgM and C1q present in arterioles. Otherwise negative. EM: Results not available.

Previously well. Nephrotic syndrome, proteinuria 5.6g per 24hrs, low C3 and C4. Immunofluorescence shows granular capillary deposition of IgA, IgM, IgG, C3 and C1, affecting all parts of all six glomeruli in the sample. The IgA staining is slightly stronger than the other immunoglobulins and C3 is slightly stronger than C1.
ANCA positive small vessel vasculitis in 2000. Was ANCA negative now ANCA positive and creatinine increasing to 200. ESR and CRP increasing. ? Vasculitis relapse.

CRF, Creatinine 300, haematuria and proteinuria, immunology pending. Immunoperoxidase showed mild mesangial IgM positivity whilst staining for IgG, C3 and IgA was negative. EM showed mild thickening and irregularity of the glomerular basement membranes but no electron dense deposits were seen. No foot process effacement was seen.

End stage COPD and Nephrotic Syndrome. Proteinuria 4.9g/24hr. Creatinine 121. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) neg. C3 <0.78 and C4 <0.04. Plasma electrophoresis IgG Lambda band. BJP present. Normal glucose tolerance. U/S both kidneys 12.5cm. Congo Red negative. Immunoperoxidase: IF: Mesangium and capillary wall IgG (traces). Light chain restriction is equivocal for Lambda.

Signs and symptoms of vasculitis, renal failure and fluid overload. History of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)/Sjogren's overlap syndrome. IF: Strong mesangial and peripheral granular deposition of IgG, A, M and C3. The hyaline intraluminal deposits strongly positive for all classes of immunoglobulin and complement.
Acute renal failure. Positive urinary sediment. No previous clinical history of note. ANCA negative. Recent E. coli urinary tract infection treated with antibiotics.

History of pancreatitis and NIDDM with retinopathy. Low grade proteinuria (0.6g/l) and creatinine of 240. Immunostaining showed a few flecks of IgM and larger amounts of C1q in a mesangial pattern and in blood vessels. Congo Red stain negative.

Six months post partum. Proteinuria 4+. Haematuria +. Uneventful pregnancy. Normotensive. Normal renal function. Negative immunology. No past history of renal disease. Immunohistochemistry - linear basement membrane and mesangial staining for C9 and IgM to lesser extent. IgA and IgG negative.

Creatinine 300. Unwell. Non-specific atypical kappa paraprotein band. ?Myeloma. No past history. BP 130/80. IF: Significant immune deposits present within glomeruli. Several casts reactive for kappa but negative for lambda.
Anaemia, renal failure and mild rouleux on blood film.
Presented with nephrotic syndrome. Normal blood pressure and normal creatinine. IF showed trace positivity for IgM. IgG, IgA, C3, C1q, Kappa and Lambda negative
Acute renal failure. Raised ESR. IF negative. EM showed no significant abnormalities.
Nephrotic (urine protein 24g per 24 hours). Creatinine has risen from 100 to 300 in three weeks. MPO positive but low titre 4.8. Active urine sediment with dysmorphic red cells. IgA and IgG negative.

Fever and myalgia for one month. Raised temperature and ESR/CRP. Blood and protein in urine. pANCA positive, MPO titre 119. Renal function normal. Immunohistochemistry for IgG, IgA, IgM, C3 and C1q was negative.

Nephrotic syndrome. 5.8g proteinuria. Normal renal function. Negative immunology. EM showed foot process effacement with numerous small/medium subepithelial electron dense deposits with variable, often small, spikes. Increase in mesangial matrix. No mesangial deposits identified.
Nephrotic, 10g. Normal creatinine. Albumin 16. ?Membranous. IF and IPx both negative.
Proteinuria. ?Cause.
Presented with microscopic haematuria and proteinuria for 8 months. Raised ASOT and IgA and decreased IgG. Immunohistochemistry for IgA, IgG and C1q were negative.
diagnosis: Linear dense deposit disease (mesangiocapillary glomerulonephritis (MCGn) type II)

clinical_details: Myeloma August 2005, rapidly progressive renal failure January 2006. antinuclear antibody (ANA), ANCA anti-GBM, all negative. Not dialysis dependent. IMF: IgM, IgG, IgA, C1q negative. C3: focal positivity in blood vessels.

Nephrotic syndrome. High cholesterol. Previous hyperthyroidism (treated with radio-iodine). COPD. Benign prostatic hypertrophy. IF: 12 glomeruli showed bright diffuse global granular capillary wall immunopositivity for IgG, light chains, C3d. Similar pattern but weaker immunopositivity also present for C3, C1q. Negative for IgA, IgM.

Known hypertension and ischaemic heart disease. Acute on chronic renal failure, creatinine increased from 140 - 1066. Haematuria, arthralgia. ANCA +, PR3. ?vasculitis. On ACE inhibitors & NSAIDS. IF showed small amount of IgA within paramesangium. Small amount of granular IgG in a peripheral location. C3 shows non-specific staining. No other immune reactants.
Arthralgia and rash. Creatinine 150, MPO >100, antinuclear antibody (ANA) +, pANCA+. Immunofluorescence: IgA/M/G, C1q, C3 all positive. EM not performed.
"Coryzal" symptoms for one month. Creatinine on presentation 608. No other significant haematological or immunological findings. IF: Negative. EM: No glomeruli.
Six weeks haemoptysis. Anti GBM positive in the blood test. Raised creatinine - 114. Immunofluorescence - IgG 3+ linear GBM staining with some C3.

Alcoholic liver disease and nephrotic syndrome with a urinary protein of 5g/24hrs. EM shows patchy effacement of podocyte foot processes and focal areas show wrinkling of basement membranes. No electron dense deposits seen
Acute Renal Failure. Creatinine 124 in May, now 837. All immunology negative. Urine blood +++ protein +++. Initial presentation before ARF was nephrotic. IF for IgA and IgM negative.
Presented with nephrotic syndrome. Urinary protein 10g/day. Rheumatoid arthritis, recent penicillamine treatment. IF showed strong capillary loop staining for IgG and C3.

Admitted with cough, pleural effusion, epistaxis. Urea 17, creatinine 310, Cr clearance 15ml/min, 1.33g proteinuria/24h. Elevated ASOT (800iu/ml) and anti-DNAase beta (1600). Immuno: C3 circulated, C1q and heavy chains negative.
Renal faiure (creatinine 540). Past history of gastric lymphoma. Proteinuria 0.2g. Normal kidney on ultrasound. IF: IgA++, C3++, IgM+, all mesangial.

Long history of IHD. Recent onset of ankle swelling with proteinuria 2g/24h. Creatinine 191 but falling to normal by time of biopsy. Cr clearance 52, albumin 29, viscosity slightly raised. Smooth muscle autoantibodies 1:160, others neg. C3 low, IgA slightly raised, C4, IgM, IgG normal. No paraprotein. IF: C3 supplied, IgG similar but weaker, A&M negative. No tissue for EM.
Renal failure developing over two months. IF for IgG, IgA, IgM, C3 and fibrin all negative.

Vomiting and haematemesis after big dose of paracetamol. Acute hepatitis (Hep. B&C negative). Acute renal failure. Creatinine 377. Enlarged kidneys - 13cm. IF: slight to moderage granular mesangial IgM and C3; small amount of granular IgA, one glom. only. EM not contributory.

Low grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma 1984; relapse 1991, cleared by mild chemo. Admitted for non-specific symptoms ??cirrhosis - liver function tests normal. Lesions on face - biopsy - confluent non-caseating granulomas (?elastolytic ?other granulomatous disease) Creatinine 61 to 108 in 3 weeks, active urinary sediment - renal biopsy. IF: patchy peripheral G, C3, C1q. M similar, weaker. Lymphocytes mostly CD20+, scattered CD3. Kappa & lambda poor quality, not informative.
Nephrotic syndrome. C3 looked like IgG. IgM, IgA negative.
Unspecified neurodegenerative disease involving autoimmune nervous system, gross oedema, proteinuria 5 gms, creatinine normal. ?Cause of nephrotic syndrome.
Acute renal failure following loin pain and GI bleed. Also Duke's C carcinoma. ?interstitial nephritis ??ATN. Now anuric. ?MPGN.
diagnosis: Vascular changes of hypertension or systemic sclerosis

clinical_details: Persistent microscopic haematuria, no proteinuria, normal renal function, normal urological exam. Top EM (1026159) is typical of patient. NB LOWER EM IS CONTROL of normal GMB at same magnification (No. 1026169).

Nephrotic 1 month, 24h protein 9.6g, alb. 26g/l, cholesterol 8.5 Oedema resolved on diuretics. BP 1324/80, creatinine 88. History of bilateral uveitis 1984. First biopsy inadequate; treated pred. 60mg/day + rimapril for 6 months. Proteinuria down only to 3 - 5g/24h so rebiopsy. Creatinine remains 89.
Acute renal failure. ESR 92mm/hr, Hb 7.7. IF negative. No tissue for EM.
Nephrotic syndrome. No underlying factors identified.

?Scleroderma. six months hypertension, SOB, joint pains, Raynaud's. Protein 0.78g/24h, nausea, peripheral oedema, +ve antinuclear antibody (ANA) 1:10,240. Serum creatinine 95 to 203 over last two weeks.
Nephrotic syndrome for one month, hypertension, microscopic haematuria. Immunoperoxidase: C3+++ in expanded mesangial areas and capillary walls. IgM+, C1q+, Fib+ in peripheral capillary walls only.
Clinical history: 'Nephrotic/Nephritic'. No other info! IF: IgG+, A+++, M+, C3+++, C1q+, all diffuse granular mesangial with some peripheral staining.
Progressive renal impairment over days. Creatinine 350, pANCA positive, MPO>1000, complains of myalgia, night sweats. History of peripheral vascular disease. IF: flecks of IgM and C3 in glomeruli.

Admitted with Hb 6 and acute anuric renal failure ?glomerulonephritis. Parents are HIV positive, he is HIV negative. No further serology available. Photo of C3 is provided. Immunoglobulins and C1q were negative.

1.9g proteinuria with haematuria, ? cause. Rheumatoid arthritis treated with NSAIDs and penicillamine. Immunohistochemistry - granular positivity basement membrane IgG IgM (both strong) and weak IgA. Complement negative.
Proteinuria (4g/24h) and hypoalbuminaemia
Lady with proteinuria and hypoalbuminaemia. History of tuberculosis; was on anti-tuberculous therapy.

Severe nephrotic syndrome with deteriorating renal function. History of congenital heart disease, seems to have RHF. Raised CRP. Serology negative. NB only on core, frozen for IF before paraffin processing. IF: C3 and IgM in mesangium and capillary walls, fibrin in a few glomerular segments. Arterioles focally positive for C3
Three week history of rash. BP normal. Creatinine 228. Has haematuria and proteinuria - ? chronic and acute changes?
Recent onset of nephrotic syndrome. Steroid resistant. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) negative.
diagnosis: Dense deposit disease (mesangiocapillary glomerulonephritis (MCGn) type II)

clinical_details: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Presented with nephrotic syndrome without any relevant medical history. Immunofluorescence was negative. Electron microscopy showed swelling of the visceral epithelial cells and global effacement of the foot processes. No deposits seen.

Diabetic for 20 years, Creatinine 169, hypertensive, haematuria for 5 years macroscopically, raised proteinuria with time. Now nephrotic. ?DM nephropathy, ?other glomerulonephritis eg membranous. Immunofluorescence all negative. Silver stain shows normal capillary basement membranes.
Nephrotic syndrome with acute renal failure. Hypoproteinaemia, proteinuria++, haematuria+++. Admitted with ?malignant hypertension.
Proteinuria, haematuria, phosphaturia, glycosuria. Probable tubulo-interstitial nephritis with uveitis. Immunohistology negative. EM: Small segments of foot process fusion. Nil else significant.

Past history of asthma and elevated eosinophils in the past, as well as hypertension. The patient was admitted with ++++proteinuria and ++haematuria. The only abnormal clinical parameter at the time of the biopsy was CRP of 17. The immunofluorescence of the case showed linear IgG and C3 staining, glomerular basement membrane antibodies were negative.
Proteinuria, haematuria, phosphaturia, glycosuria. Probable tubulo-interstitial nephritis with uveitis. Immunohistology negative. EM: Small segments of foot process fusion. Nil else significant.
Blood and proteinuria, renal bruit, raised creatinine and calcium.
History of chronic alcohol abuse; acute renal failure with anuria and haemodialysis; microhaematuria; leucocyturia.
Waldenstrom's macroglobulinaemia. Creatinine 650. Congo Red strongly positive with typical birefringence. No special stain available.
Non-functioning, refluxing kidney. Nephrectomy done. Two of the sections of the kidney submitted.
diagnosis: Refluxing kidney with duplex urether and renal dysplasia

clinical_details: Left renal tumour. Gross: A nephrectomy specimen 11 x 5 x 5 cm with 6 cm of attached ureter, weight 248 g. There was an irregular nodular yellowish and cream coloured tumour in the lower pole and hilum measuring 7 cm maximum diameter.

Incidental mass left kidney when scanned for aneurysm. CT suggestive of renal malignancy. Radical nephrectomy (left). Laparoscopic. Gross: Radical nephrectomy including adrenal gland. The kidney (without attached fat) measures 12.5cm in length x 6cm in depth x 5cm in width. In its mid portion, slightly towards the inferior pole, is a tan brown, variegated tumour with focal fibrosis and haemorrhage, measuring 4.2cm in maximum diameter. It appears still confined within the renal capsule although it is bulging the surface. Special stains showed that the tumour cells do not contain glycogen or mucin (PAS negative). Rare scattered cells and occasional entrapped renal tubules are CK7 +ve, but the majority of tumour cells do not stain for CK7. Hale's colloidal iron is negative with the exception of focal weak/fine dust-like staining in rare cells. CD10 is negative except for very focal cytoplasmic staining (no membrane staining seen). Vimentin and CK20 are negative.

Right adrenal mass seen on ultrasound in a patient with previous TCC bladder. Initial needle biopsy was performed and a diagnosis was suggested. Patient was investigated further and the adrenal was removed. Gland weighed 25g. Cut surface showed a well defined unencapsulated mass 30 mm across. Synaptophysin, chromogranin and NSE are positive. Occasional scattered cells show positive nuclear staining for S100 protein. Negative cytokeratin and epithelial membrane antigen.
Spherical tumour in mid zone of left kidney 4.5cm in diameter. Tumour is apparently within renal capsule but bulges beyond renal outline. Cut surface is tan and uniform.

Resistant hypertension requiring multiple drug treatments. Reduced renin and elevated aldosterone. Depressed potassium levels. Equivocal CT images suggest abnormal right adrenal gland with possible adenoma.
Admitted with acute renal failure. ?Acute tubular necrosis. Patient is a homeless male living rough.
diagnosis: Crystal nephropathy (oxalosis) related to ethylene glycol ingestion

clinical_details: Previous myeloma. Haematuria. Renal mass. ?RCC, ?lymphoma, ?plasmacytoma.
Renal transplant. Small lesion upper pole.
Excision of right renal tumour.
diagnosis: Conventional renal cell carcinoma / adrenal nodular cortical hyperplasia

clinical_details: Unusual pathology of the right kidney. Renal pelvis mass.
diagnosis: Renal cell carcinoma, conventional type, grade 4

clinical_details: Renal cell carcinoma
6cm lower pole renal tumour. Looks like RCC on CT scan. Specimen: Right kidney. Macro: Kidney with cystic mass in lower pole 5.5cm maximum. No obvious invasion of renal capsule, pelvis or renal vein.
Incidental finding during investigation of colorectal cancer. Left kidney unilocular cyst 17cm in upper pole. Interior is lined with nodules of fawn tissue and blood clot.
Vimentin, CD10 and RCC negative. Positive hales colloidal iron. Specimen: Kidney. Macro: 6cm tumour involving upper pole of left kidney, invading capsule.
Lesion lower pole kidney

A 49 year-old man developed a lower back pain. He was found to have a large mass in his left kidney. The kidney was removed, which contained a well-defined mass (6.4cm) in the lower pole with extension to the perinephric adipose tissue.

A 63 year-old woman was found to have a large cystic lesion in her left kidney. A radical nephrectomy was performed. The kidney was remarkable for a well-circumscribed 5.5cm large cystic mass in the cortex and medulla. Cystic space contained clotted blood.
Nephrectomy for CT diagnosed RCC. During op, tumour found to extend to diaphragm. Frozen section to confirm presence of tumour.
Partial nephrectomy for renal tumour. Section from a right renal mass.
Nephrectomy.

PUJ - abscess/?tumour. Immuno: ?CD68+, AE1/AE2-, S100-. The kidney measures 100 x 100 x 70mm. The external surface showed scarring. On slicing, the pelvicalyceal system is dilated and filled with pus. There are extensive ?inflammatory changes.
Right-sided retroperitoneal mass extending from kidney through liver capsule - ? retroperitoneal sarcoma. Core biopsy.
Large renal tumour - omental nodules fround during laparotomy (send for frozen section) - ? metastatic renal carcinoma.
Nephrectomy for Polycystic kidney, solid area adjacent to cyst.

Renal transplant. Transplant 3/12 previously. Date pyelonephritis on biopsy at 18 days. CMV colitis at 1 month. Macro: Kidney 120 mm in length. Pale granular material present on cordial surface and extetnds through the cortex in a linear pattern on surface.

Solid lesion middle of right kidney, radiologically renal cancer. Specimen: Right kidney. Macro: Right kidney - nephrectomy specimen. Kidney itself measures 95 x 50 x 34 mm. Shows a yellow-grey rounded tumour 32 mm diameter centrally.

No clinical details provided. Specimen: Partial nephrectomy. Macro: Partial nephrectomy specimen, not including renal pelvis, ureter and vascular structure with a circumscribed yellow tumour 30x20x30mm, focally close to capsular surface. Immuno: CK+ve . Focal CD10+ve. CD117 -ve.
5 cm tumour in right kidney. Right laparoscopic radical nephrectomy. Section of tumour
Nephrectomy for renal carcinoma
Kidney
Nephrotic syndrome. Steroid dependent. Renal function normal.
Nephrotic syndrome. Renal function within normal limits.
Nephrotic syndrome, non-responsive to steroids.
Nephrotic syndrome. Immunology negative aside from mild elevation of ANA. Possible minimal change?
Proteinuria with haematuria. Family history of renal failure.
Known diagnosis of lupus nephritis. Previously treated with various agents. Poor compliance. Now worsening renal function. Blood / protein on dipstick. Exclude other aetiologies.
Long-standing proteinuria. Acute admission with malignant hypertension and impaired renal function.
diagnosis: Dense deposit disease with severe chronic damage

clinical_details: Haemato-proteinuria. Proteinuria in the nephrotic range. Stage 4 chronic kidney disease and hypertension.
Nephrotic syndrome with oedema and ascites. Immunology negative. No systemic symptoms. ?Cause.

Chronic kidney disease. Admitted with acute kidney injury. Now anuric and on dialysis. Also type 2 diabetic with diabetic retinopathy. Hypotensive. Underwent angiography. Immunology negative. Contrast induced nephropathy? ischaemic injury due to hypotension? diabetic nephropathy?
diagnosis: Diabetic nephropathy with acute tubular injury

clinical_details: Acute kidney injury. On haemodialysis. Significant co-morbidities including cerebral infarcts, GI bleeds and previous DVT. Immunology negative.
Frank haematuria and abdominal pain. Proteinuria of 3.5g in 24 hours. Negative immunology.
Declining renal function. pANCA positive. MPO over 500. Anti-GBM negative. No systemic symptoms.

Acute kidney injury. History of treated hypertension. Generally unwell with suspected respiratory tract infection for some time. Hospitalised after deterioration. Developed renal failure thus treated with renal replacement therapy. Anti-GBM positive. Anti-GBM disease?
Acute kidney injury now on dialysis. Previous medical history of diabetes and hypertension. Immunology: ANCA+
Trace of blood and protein in urine. Interstitial nephritis? vasculitis?
Acute kidney injury. Proteinuria. ?Cause.
pANCA positive. Presented with weight loss.
Chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, T2 diabetes. Arteriopath - triple vessel angiography. Ischaemia in peripheral limbs.
Known systemic lupus erythematosus. Proteinuria. Normal function. Has had treatment.
Patient with anaemia and hypertension. Developed proteinuruia. Immunology and Bence-Jones protein negative.
Dialysis dependent renal failure. Haemolysis and reduced platelets. ?HUS
Transplant with ABO / HLA incompatible donor. Sudden rise in creatinine. Rejection or tacrolimus toxicity?
Donation after cardiac death to polycystic disease recipient. Good initial function now deteriorating.
diagnosis: Severe arteriopathy and arteriolar hyalinosis with mild to moderate chronic tubulo-interstitial and glomerular damage

clinical_details: Polycystic disease. End stage renal failure. ABO incompatible. Delayed graft function. On dialysis. ?Rejection
ABO incompatible donor. Rising creatinine. Haemolytic anaemia on blood film. ?Rejection.
Renal transplant. Previous early rejection.
diagnosis: Suspicious for acute T-cell mediated rejection

clinical_details: Thalassaemia. Renal transplant. Rapid rise in creatinine.
Renal transplant 3 years ago. Cause of renal failure unknown. Previous episodes of rejection. Rising creatinine.
Chronic pyelonephritis. Transplanted. Previous episodes of cellular rejection.
Previous anti-GBM disease. Transplanted. Deteriorating. Previous biopsy - rejection, ATN and tubulitis.
diagnosis: Acute antibody mediated rejection Grade II

clinical_details: Chronic pyelonephritis progressed to renal failure. Hypertension. Transplanted 10 years ago. Rising creatinine and proteinuria.
Familial renal disease. Transplanted recently. Creatinine rising.
Reflux nephropathy. Transplanted. ABO incompatible. Fibromuscular dysplasia of donor arteries. BK nephropathy. Decline in function despite treatment. ?On-going BK nephropathy / rejection?
End stage renal failure due to mesangiocapillary glomerulonephritis (type 1). Previous treated with haemodialysis. Transplant. Now creatinine rising. Adenovirus on PCR.
IgA nephropathy progressed to end stage renal failure. Transplanted. Rising creatinine. Proteinuria.
End stage renal failure. IgA nephropathy. Renal transplant 2006. Proteinuria but otherwise stable function.
diagnosis: Hypertensive vasculopathy with secondary focal segmental glomerular sclerosis

clinical_details: Transplanted 2007. Cause of renal failure - IgA nephropathy. eGFR decreasing / increasing proteinuria. Cause?
End stage renal failure. Transplanted 2001. Rising creatinine. Possible relapse of microscopic polyangiitis?
diagnosis: Crescentic glomerulonephritis (immune complex glomerulonephritis of uncertain aetiology)

clinical_details: Steroid resistant nephrotic syndrome
Nephrotic syndrome, proteinuria 13g/day, serum albumin 20.
Nephrotic syndrome. Previous minimal change diagnosis.
3 month history of nephrotic syndrome
Haematoproteinuria. Background of hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, diabetes. Chronic renal failure. Creatinine 200.
Persistent proteinuria. Impaired renal function
Nephritis ?cause
Haematoproteinuria with rising creatinine
Stage 2 chronic kidney disease with hypertension and proteinuria.
Haematuria.

Rash and diarrhoea. Eosinophilia. Haematuria
IgG lambda monoclone. Acute kidney injury. Creatinine 400.
Right radical nephrectomy for kidney mass. Macro: Nephrectomy specimen with a large uniform tan-coloured mass 10 x 9 x 6 cm. Specimen: Kidney.

Biopsy proven TCC right ureter -poorly functioning kidney - right nephrouretectomy. Specimen: Nephrouretectomy. Macro: 402 g specimen of kidney with fat overall 135 x 55 85 mm with 180 mm ureter, the kidney itself 97 x 60 x 40 mm. No residual ureteric lesion seen macroscopically. Only finding was incidental 10 x 7 mm well circumscribed yellow lesion renal cortex showing focal haemorrhagic areas. (Note - no residual TCC either macroscopically or microscopically in ureter- presumed removed by previous biopsy). Immuno: Vimentin, CD10, CK7, RCC, Carbonic anhydrase, EMA - positive.

Right renal tumour. Specimen: Right partial nephrectomy. Macro: A partial nephrectomy specimen measuring 50 x 45 x 21 mm. On sectioning, there is a dark brown, well circumscribed tumour mass measuring 23 x 19 x 16 mm. Immuno: Diffuse membranous staining for CD117, CK 7 mainly negative, CD 10 negative, vimentin negative. Hale's colloidal iron stain equivocal.

End stage renal failure on dialysis. Acquired cystic kidney disease and had been followed up. On last scan a more solid lesion was seen. Specimen: Left nephrectomy. Macro: Atrophic and scarred kidney with multiple cysts, 5-35mm in size. Within the largest cyst was a solid lesion, 18mm, which is represented on submitted section. Immuno: None.
diagnosis: Renal cell carcinoma - Acquired cystic disease associated RCC

clinical_details: Tumour right kidney on scan. Partial nephrectomy. Tumour 18mm diameter.

Renal tumour. Immunohistochemistry shows positivity for cytokeratin AE1-3, EMA, CD10 and CD117. Negative for CD15 and Vimentin. Specimen: Renal biopsy. Macro: An upper pole tumour 17cm in maximum dimension. Cut surface is tan-yellow with a central fibrotic area.
Incidental tumour in the left lower pole of kidney. Specimen: Section of tumour. Macro: None provided.
Mass in left kidney

17mm lesion in right kidney, disrupted during excision. Representative section.

2week H/0 fever and malaise.

Right nephroureterectomy for ?TCC ureter. Specimen: Nodular expansion of ureter over a distance of 40mm, up to a thickness of 15mm. On slicing, wall markedly thickened with collapsed lumen. Special stains/IHC results: CD10 is focally positive. GATA-3 is negative in these areas (but stains the urothelium of the ureter).

Small lower pole right renal tumour. Procedure laparoscopic right partial nephrectomy. Specimen: Right partial nephrectomy - A 10 g piece of kidney tissue measuring 30 x 26 x 28 mm within which is a smooth, cream, exophytic tumour measuring 26 x 24 x 22 mm. There is a ring of parenchyma around the tumour measuring up to 4 mm in thickness. The cut surface is smooth and white. No confluent necrosis or haemorrhagic areas are seen.

Special stains/IHC results: Positive: PAX8, WT1 and CD57. Negative: CK7, racemase, CD10, EMA.

Mixed solid cystic upper pole renal tumour. Specimen: Left partial nephrectomy.

Special stains/IHC results: CK7 (+), CD10 (-), racemase (-).
diagnosis: Clear cell papillary renal cell carcinoma

Interstitial lung shadowing more towards bases. Wedge biopsy of lung. What is the distribution of the pathology? Is it predominantly fibrotic or cellular or a mix? What compartment is the pathology predominantly in (interstitial vs airspace)? What is the term used for the slightly myxoid areas containing plump spindle cells beneath the alveolar surface (look slightly pale and purple)? What are these spindle cells laying down?
diagnosis: Fibrosing interstitial pneumonia with usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern

clinical_details: Shortness of breath 3 years initially steroid responsive. Wedge biopsy of lung. What is the distribution of the pathology? What is the predominant pathological abnormality (cellular vs fibrotic)? What compartment is the pathology in predominantly (airspaces vs interstitium)? From these 3 questions how could you summarise what pattern of lung disease we see here?

Smoker. Shortness of breath. CT looks emphysematous. Wedge biopsy of lung. At low power: where are the abnormalities? how would you describe them? At 35 yrs old are they more likely to related or unrelated? (Loaded question alert!) If related what might link them?

Smoker with recurrent pneumothoraces. Wedge biopsy of lung. What is the predominant distribution of the pathology? How would you describe the cells in the nodular lesions? What might they stain with immunohistochemically? One nodule looks slightly different - how would you describe it? What are the background bronchioles like?
Smoker with recurrent pneumothoraces. Wedge biopsy of lung. Is this normal? (loaded question!) If not where is the abnormality? What are the main cell types involved?

Works in aviary. Upper zone predominant ground glass lung infiltrates on CT with some air trapping. Wedge biopsy of lung. Is this predominantly a cellular or fibrotic process? What compartment is the main abnormality (interstitium vs alveolar)? What is the distribution?

Rash on face and joint problems. Lung infiltrates on imaging. Wedge biopsy of lung. Questions to consider initially, with a potential interstitial lung disease, are: What compartment/s is the pathology in (ie airspaces / interstitium)? What is the distribution on low power (eg patchy / widespread / subpleural / paraseptal / peribronchiolar)? Is the abnormality mainly cellular, mainly fibrotic or a combination? (If fibrotic, is it established or ongoing (ie organising pneumonia/fibroblastic activity?))
Migratory fluffy infiltrates on xray. Lung wedge biopsy. What are the main pathological abnormalities? What is their location and distribution?
diagnosis: Organising Pneumonia in keeping with bronchiolitis obliterans organising pneumonia (BOOP)in the right clinical context.
